Flow matching and Schrödinger bridges define probability paths, yet their inference grids are usually heuristic or inherited from one-endpoint diffusion. We derive a conditional-marginal entropy-rate objective for bridge-aware discretization, separating endpoint-conditioned bridge geometry from marginal flow evolution, and use it to build a training-free entropic inference-time scheduler from first principles. For Gaussian Brownian bridges this rate is closed-form and U-shaped, motivating boundary-heavy nonuniform grids. On trained two-dimensional bridge/flow models, the estimated profile recovers the predicted shape and improves 10-step ODE-Heun MMD over linear by 18.1%, with a paired 22.7% SDE-Heun improvement in the same low-NFE sweep. On EDM/CIFAR-10, the entropic time-discretization gives the best tested five-step FID (186.3 \pm 4.0 versus 200.5 \pm 2.9 for linear and 238.0 \pm 5.3 for cosine). On AlphaFlow protein generation, entropic conditional-marginal (cond-marg) scheduling shows advantage in low-NFE regimes on both CAMEO22 and ATLAS benchmarks. These results support entropy-rate scheduling as a practical low-budget allocation signal for high-dimensional bridge and flow samplers.

</details>


### [350] [Navigating Potholes with Geometry-Aware Sharpness Minimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16134)
*Simon Dufort-Labbé,Mehrab Hamidi,Razvan Pascanu,Ioannis Mitliagkas,Damien Scieur,Aristide Baratin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LLQR+SAM enhances the Sharpness-aware minimization (SAM) method by incorporating a learned preconditioner to better account for the loss landscape geometry, improving performance on various benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SAM focuses on finding flat minima via perturbations to address sharpness in loss landscapes, but it does not consider the underlying geometry of the loss, limiting its effectiveness.

Method: Introduces LLQR+SAM, which combines SAM with LLQR's learned preconditioner updated via a slow-moving exponential average to encapsulate the loss geometry, facilitating two-timescale optimization: slow preconditioner updates and fast SAM perturbations.

Result: LLQR+SAM outperforms both standalone SAM and LLQR on multiple vision and sequence modeling tasks by effectively balancing loss geometry learning and curvature probing.

Conclusion: By combining slow updates to model loss geometry with quick sharpness corrections, LLQR+SAM provides improved optimization, demonstrating that these approaches are complementary.

Abstract: Sharpness-aware minimization (SAM) encourages flat minima by perturbing parameters along directions of high loss curvature, but treats all parameter directions uniformly, ignoring the underlying loss geometry. We introduce LLQR+SAM, which combines SAM with a learned preconditioner obtained from the recently proposed LLQR framework, a second-order method that recasts steepest descent as a layerwise linear-quadratic regulator problem. The preconditioner is updated sparsely and maintained as a slow exponential moving average, so it captures a smoothed, low-resolution picture of the loss landscape geometry. The SAM perturbation then operates on top of this learned geometry, probing curvature at a faster timescale. We show that this two-timescale structure is not merely a computational convenience: theoretically, the preconditioner amplifies the SAM escape signal in directions that are flat under the average geometry but locally sharp (potholes). Wide, flat basins, by contrast, remain stable. Empirically, LLQR+SAM gives consistent gains over both SAM and LLQR alone across standard vision and sequence modeling benchmarks, supporting the view that slow learned geometry and fast sharpness correction are genuinely complementary.

</details>


### [351] [Surrogate Neural Architecture Codesign Package (SNAC-Pack)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16138)
*Jason Weitz,Dmitri Demler,Benjamin Hawks,Aaron Wang,Nhan Tran,Javier Duarte*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces SNAC-Pack, a framework for automating hardware-aware neural architecture design and FPGA deployment with significant resource optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing NAS methods fail to properly address hardware costs and dimensions, particularly for FPGA, leading to inefficient designs when applied to real-world hardware constraints.

Method: The framework combines multi-objective global search using Optuna and NSGA-II with hardware surrogate models for resource estimation and a local search stage involving quantization-aware training and iterative pruning.

Result: SNAC-Pack significantly reduced FPGA resource usage and search time while achieving performance on par or superior to strong task-specific baselines.

Conclusion: SNAC-Pack enables efficient and automated design of hardware-aware neural architectures for FPGA, bridging the gap between NAS and real-world hardware constraints.

Abstract: Neural architecture search (NAS) is a powerful approach for automating model design, but existing methods often optimize for accuracy alone or rely on proxy metrics such as bit operations (BOPs) that correlate poorly with hardware cost. This gap is particularly large for FPGA deployment, where cost is dominated by a multi-dimensional budget of lookup tables, DSPs, flip-flops, BRAM, and latency. We present the Surrogate Neural Architecture Codesign Package (SNAC-Pack), an open-source AutoML framework for hardware-aware neural architecture codesign and end-to-end FPGA deployment. SNAC-Pack runs a multi-objective global search with Optuna and NSGA-II, loading trials to a shared SQLite store that enables parallel workers across compute nodes. A hardware surrogate model outputs per-trial resource and latency estimates, avoiding the synthesis cost that would otherwise dominate the search loop. A local search stage then applies quantization-aware training (QAT) together with iterative magnitude pruning in a combined compression loop, after which the final model is synthesized to FPGA firmware via the hls4ml Python library. A YAML configuration and an optional agentic frontend let users run the pipeline on new datasets without modifying the framework. We demonstrate SNAC-Pack on jet classification at the Large Hadron Collider and superconducting qubit readout, discovering compact architectures that match or exceed strong baselines on the task metric while reducing FPGA resource utilization and, in the qubit readout case, reducing the design space exploration process from months of manual fine-tuning to hours of automated search.

</details>


### [352] [Entropic Auto-Encoding via Implicit Free-Energy Minimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16164)
*Hazhir Aliahmadi,Irina Babayan,Greg van Anders*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper presents Entropic Autoencoders (EAEs) to combat posterior collapse in VAEs by using entropy to derive latent priors and achieve superior representations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Variational autoencoders (VAEs) frequently suffer from posterior collapse, where latent variables are ignored due to prior-driven optimization toward uninformative representations.

Method: The method introduces Entropic Autoencoders (EAEs), where reconstruction loss is the sole objective and entropy implicitly generates latent priors using a free energy-minimizing ensemble of encoders.

Result: EAEs mitigate posterior collapse by learning non-Gaussian, multimodal latent distributions that yield diverse and data-consistent generations, preserving data structure.

Conclusion: EAEs effectively address the limitations of VAEs, showcasing their ability to uncover structure and hierarchical distinctions in datasets like reaction-diffusion processes, MNIST, and CelebA.

Abstract: Despite their ubiquity, variational autoencoders (VAEs) inherently suffer from posterior collapse, a failure mode in which latent variables are effectively ignored. This failure arises because explicit prior imposition drives optimization toward loss landscape regions corresponding to uninformative latent representations. Here, we introduce Entropic Autoencoders (EAEs), a framework in which reconstruction loss is the only explicit objective, and entropy generates the latent variables' prior implicitly through a free energy-minimizing ensemble of encoders. This ensemble biases learning toward high-volume regions of near-optimal solutions, while decoder updates direct the search trajectories toward informative latent representations. We demonstrate that EAEs mitigate posterior collapse by learning non-Gaussian, multimodal latent distributions that yield diverse, data-consistent generations and preserve different forms of underlying structure in the data. As a proof-of-concept, we show that an EAE captures a superposition of the known low-dimensional dynamics of a reaction-diffusion process. Then, we show that an EAE identifies implicit categorical distinctions in MNIST latent representations, and displays a hierarchical understanding of facial structure on the CelebA dataset, from an "all-human" face to individual-dependent features.

</details>


### [353] [BAPR: Bayesian amnesic piecewise-robust reinforcement learning for non-stationary continuous control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16170)
*Yifan Zhang,Liang Zheng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces BAPR (Bayesian Amnesic Piecewise-Robust SAC), combining Bayesian Online Change Detection with ensemble reinforcement learning for adaptive conservatism in piecewise stationary control systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Control systems often experience stable periods interspersed with abrupt changes, posing a challenge for RL methods to balance adaptivity and robustness.

Method: BAPR integrates Bayesian Online Change Detection and robust ensemble RL, utilizing a belief-weighted Bellman operator with formal machine-verified error bounds and contraction analysis.

Result: The approach ensures adaptive conservatism, smooth confidence adjustment post-change detection, mode-aware representations, and formal guarantees via Lean 4 verification.

Conclusion: BAPR successfully addresses the dilemma of robust versus adaptive RL under dynamic regimes, providing mode-switch resilience and theoretically grounded reliability.

Abstract: Real-world control systems frequently operate under \emph{piecewise stationary} conditions, where dynamics remain stable for extended periods before undergoing abrupt regime changes. Standard robust RL methods face a fundamental dilemma: a globally conservative policy wastes performance during stable periods, while a locally adaptive policy risks catastrophic failure when the regime changes undetected.
  We propose \textbf{BAPR} (Bayesian Amnesic Piecewise-Robust SAC), which unifies Bayesian Online Change Detection (BOCD) with robust ensemble RL. The BAPR operator -- a convex combination of mode-conditional Bellman operators weighted by a frozen belief distribution -- is a $γ$-contraction. A complementary counterexample, machine-verified in Lean~4, establishes a \emph{sharp boundary}: when beliefs depend on the Q-function, the contraction factor becomes $γ+ λΔ$ (where $Δ$ is the mode reward gap), and contraction fails exactly when $γ+ λΔ\geq 1$. We derive a \emph{component-wise} formal error budget for the abstract operator -- every component machine-verified -- bounding post-switch recovery; the budget applies to the abstract mode-mixture operator and inherits to the implemented shared-critic algorithm only through the frozen-parameter design intuition. All results are formally verified with no \texttt{sorry} (1,145 lines across 3 Lean~4 files, 22 machine-verified theorems). BOCD drives an adaptive conservatism mechanism: the policy becomes maximally conservative after detected change-points and smoothly relaxes as confidence grows, with detection delay $O(\log(1/δ))$. A context-conditioning module trained via RMDM loss provides mode-aware representations from simulator-provided mode IDs at training time and requires no mode labels at deployment.

</details>


### [354] [Imitation learning for clinical decision support in pediatric ECMO](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16175)
*Fateme Golivand,Michael Skinner,Saurabh Mathur,Ameet Soni,Phillip Reeder,Kristian Kersting,Lakshmi Raman,Sriraam Natarajan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper frames pediatric ECMO decision-making as an imitation learning problem and tests a transformer-based approach (TabPFN), showing it outperforms traditional methods like XGBoost and MLPs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the high complexity and data scarcity in pediatric ECMO and improve clinical decision support systems by modeling interventions effectively.

Method: The authors employ imitation learning to model decision-making from observational data and compare TabPFN, a transformer-based algorithm, against traditional methods like XGBoost and MLPs using real-world pediatric ECMO data.

Result: TabPFN consistently outperforms the traditional approaches, showing promise as a baseline for clinician-behavior modeling in this domain.

Conclusion: The study supports TabPFN as an effective tool for pediatric ECMO decision support, demonstrating its superior performance over classical methods.

Abstract: Pediatric critical care is a dynamic, high-stakes process involving constant monitoring and adjustments in life-saving treatments. Modeling these interventions is crucial for effective decision support. To address the challenges of high complexity and data scarcity in pediatric Extracorporeal Membrane Oxygenation (ECMO), we frame clinical decision-making as learning to act from trajectories, i.e., imitation learning that learns action models from observational data, with a key feature that actions are not directly observed. We consider TabPFN, a recent transformer-based approach for tabular data, and traditional baselines including XGBoost and Multi-Layer Perceptrons(MLPs) on real-world pediatric ECMO data to learn the action models. We find that the TabPFN-based approach consistently outperforms these classical baselines, supporting its use as a strong clinician-behavior baseline for pediatric ECMO decision support.

</details>


### [355] [Hypothesis-driven construction of mesoscopic dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16211)
*Zhuoyuan Li,Aiqing Zhu,Qianxiao Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a learning paradigm to model mesoscopic dynamics with theoretical guarantees, validated through empirical tests.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to simplify modeling of complex systems like multiscale dynamics where traditional methods involving fixed equations struggle.

Method: They introduce a unified framework based on a generalized Onsager principle to learn mesoscopic dynamics within a constrained hypothesis class, providing mathematical guarantees and empirical validation using data.

Result: The framework yields accurate, robust, interpretable models and diagnostics for dynamics from PDEs and microscopic chain models.

Conclusion: The approach effectively learns and interprets mesoscopic dynamics, serving as an improved tool for modeling complex systems.

Abstract: Traditional scientific modeling typically begins with fixed, instance-wise effective equations and then carries out equation-specific analysis and computation, a procedure that becomes exceptionally challenging in complex applications such as multiscale systems. We propose an alternative paradigm by learning mesoscopic dynamics within a mathematically constrained hypothesis class. Building upon a generalized Onsager principle, we introduce a unified framework encompassing both dissipative and conservative mesoscopic dynamics. We establish uniform and a priori theoretical guarantees, including global well-posedness, asymptotic stability, unique factorization identifiability, and discrete energy dissipation, applicable to all spatio-temporal evolution equations within this hypothesis class prior to all learning stages. Data from each problem instance is then used to guide the identification of members within our hypothesis class, giving rise to accurate, robust and interpretable dynamical models. We empirically validate this framework on both data from continuum PDE models as a check, and on data arising from microscopic chain models for which exact meso-scale models are unknown. The proposed approach not only acts as an effective dynamics learner, but also offers vital interpretable diagnostics of the underlying physics.

</details>


### [356] [Dynamics-Level Watermarking of Flow Matching Models with Random Codes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16239)
*Shuchan Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a novel watermarking strategy by embedding signals into the learned dynamics of generative models instead of weights or outputs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a watermarking method for generative models that doesn't interfere with output quality while ensuring robust message recovery and security.

Method: Key-dependent perturbations are applied during training to a flow matching model's learned dynamics, allowing for message recovery via black-box queries without altering the generated distribution.

Result: Experiments on MNIST and CIFAR-10 demonstrate successful message recovery, maintained generation quality, and secure decoding without the key.

Conclusion: This dynamics-based watermarking approach is effective, secure, and non-intrusive for generative models.

Abstract: We introduce a dynamics-level approach to watermarking generative models. Rather than embedding signals into model weights or outputs, we embed the watermark directly into the learned continuous dynamics -- the velocity field of a flow matching model. We formulate this as random coding over a continuous channel: a key-dependent perturbation is added during training, and the message is recovered at detection time from black-box queries. The perturbation is designed to leave the generated distribution unchanged. Experiments on MNIST and CIFAR-10 across different architectures confirm reliable message recovery, preserved generation quality, and chance-level decoding accuracy without the secret key.

</details>


<div id='cs.NE'></div>

# cs.NE [[Back]](#toc)

### [357] [Bridging Silicon and the Hippocampus: Algebro-Deterministic Memory "VaCoAl" as a Substrate for Vector-HaSH and TEM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15652)
*Hiroyuki Chuma,Kanji Otsuka,Yoichi Sato*

Main category: cs.NE

TL;DR: The paper presents VaCoAl, a deterministic hyperdimensional memory architecture to unify computational neuroscience frameworks and explain hippocampal and entorhinal-circuit mechanisms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To integrate separate computational neuroscience models, Vector-HaSH and TEM, and provide a unified algebraic framework for hippocampal-entorhinal circuit functionalities.

Method: VaCoAl leverages Galois-field deterministic diffusion as an alternative to random projections in neural models, validating algebraic predictions and memory fidelity decay mechanisms.

Result: VaCoAl achieves quasi-orthogonality with better reproducibility and fidelity modeling, aligning computational constraints with hippocampal pathways.

Conclusion: The architecture provides predictive models for neural replay phenomena, aligns with evolutionary insights, and bridges neural computation and hyperdimensional engineering.

Abstract: Vector-HaSH and the Tolman-Eichenbaum Machine (TEM) propose that the hippocampal-entorhinal circuit factorizes content from a prestructured grid-cell scaffold and supports compositional memory via ripple-mediated replay. Human iEEG shows that hippocampal sharp-wave ripples (SWRs) gate episodic recall, ripple-locked cortical reactivation recapitulates encoding-time patterns, and multi-hop replay fidelity decays multiplicatively along sequence length. These literatures have advanced in parallel without a shared algebraic object. We show that VaCoAl, an algebro-deterministic hyperdimensional memory architecture built from Galois-field LFSRs, supplies that object. Specifically, deterministic Galois-field diffusion provides a substrate-level alternative to Vector-HaSH's random scaffold-to-hippocampus projection that satisfies the same quasi-orthogonality requirement, with matched second-moment statistics, stronger avalanche behavior, and bit-exact reproducibility. The path-integral Confidence Ratio $CR_2$, the product of per-step $CR_1$ values along an $n$-hop chain, is the natural functional form for multi-hop replay-fidelity decay under conditional independence of per-step reactivation, providing the first algebraically tractable model of reported multiplicative decay. STDP-like path selection in VaCoAl follows from architectural demands -- similarity preservation, compositional reversibility, and bounded-frontier search -- that also constrain hippocampal computation. We further argue that VaCoAl operating regimes share architectural commitments with the EC--CA3 and EC--DG--CA3 pathways, motivating an energy-capacity-plasticity reading of why both are conserved across $>$520 Myr of evolution and primate dentate-gyrus elaboration. We prove formal correspondences, derive testable iEEG predictions, and bridge computational neuroscience and hyperdimensional engineering.

</details>


### [358] [General-Purpose Co-Evolutionary Construction of Parallel Algorithm Portfolios for Multi-Objective Binary Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15729)
*Zhiyuan Wang,Shengcai Liu,Shaofeng Zhang,Ke Tang*

Main category: cs.NE

TL;DR: The paper proposes a domain-agnostic approach (DACMO) for constructing parallel algorithm portfolios for multi-objective binary optimization problems (MOBOPs), featuring neural instance representation and LLM-based operator generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There was no general-purpose method available for constructing parallel algorithm portfolios for MOBOPs, necessitating the development of DACMO.

Method: The authors introduced DACMO with two innovations: a neural instance representation system for scalable problem handling and LLM-based automatic operator generation for PAP construction.

Result: DACMO was tested on four MOBOP classes and demonstrated competitive or superior results compared to classic and state-of-the-art methods, achieving domain generality.

Conclusion: DACMO provides a strong, domain-agnostic framework for general-purpose PAP construction in MOBOPs, outperforming traditional and specialized approaches in many cases.

Abstract: Despite recent progress in constructing generalizable parallel algorithm portfolios (PAPs), no general-purpose approach is yet available for multi-objective binary optimization problems (MOBOPs). To fill this gap, this paper proposes domain-agnostic co-evolution of parameterized search for multi-objective binary optimization~(DACMO), which features two technical innovations. First, we propose a neural instance representation architecture that decouples domain-invariant and instance-specific features, enabling class-consistent instance generation across varying dimensions without problem-specific instance generators. Second, we introduce LLM-based automatic search operator generation into PAP construction, extending the search space from parameter tuning of predefined templates to operator-level algorithm design. We evaluate DACMO on four representative MOBOP classes to demonstrate its effectiveness as a general-purpose PAP construction method: the multi-objective match max problem~(MMMP), the multi-objective knapsack problem~(MKP), the multi-objective contamination control problem (MCCP), and the multi-objective complementary influence maximization problem~(MCIMP). Experimental results show that DACMO can be directly applied to all four problem classes without modification, outperforms PAPs built from classic MOEA templates, and achieves performance comparable to a privileged state-of-the-art baseline that relies on manually designed problem-specific instance generators, while outperforming it on two of the four evaluated problem classes.

</details>


### [359] [Structure Abstraction and Generalization in a Hippocampal-Entorhinal Inspired World Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15733)
*Tianqiu Zhang,Muyang Lyu,Xiao Liu,Si Wu*

Main category: cs.NE

TL;DR: The paper introduces a hierarchical brain-inspired model to replicate how the brain abstracts structures from high-dimensional experiences. It focuses on the HPC-MEC circuit for better structural generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to understand how the brain, specifically the hippocampal-entorhinal circuit, concurrently extracts abstract structures from continuous and complex dynamics, a process not yet well understood.

Method: A hierarchical model is proposed that includes an inverse model for structural abstraction and an HPC-MEC-based coupling model. The latter dissociates conceptual structures in MEC from episodic scenes in HPC, leveraging velocity-driven path integration and transformation dynamics.

Result: The proposed framework demonstrates the ability to perform structural abstraction and achieve robust structural generalization across contexts, using predictive models for scene dynamics.

Conclusion: This framework exemplifies how brain-inspired models, through self-supervised learning, aid in acquiring reusable knowledge and provide insights into the representation of structures in the brain.

Abstract: Humans abstract experiences into structured representations to facilitate pattern inference and knowledge transfer. While the hippocampal-entorhinal (HPC-MEC) circuit is known to represent both spatial and conceptual spaces, the mechanisms for concurrently extracting abstract structures from continuous, high-dimensional dynamics remain poorly understood. We propose a brain-inspired hierarchical model that simultaneously infers latent transitions and constructs a predictive visual world model. Our architecture employs an inverse model for structural extraction alongside an HPC-MEC coupling model that dissociates relational structures (MEC) from integrated episodic scenes (HPC). Using primitive transformation dynamics as a benchmark, we demonstrate the model's capacity for structural abstraction. By leveraging velocity-driven path integration, the framework enables robust prediction and structural reuse across diverse contexts, thereby achieving structural generalization. This work provides a novel computational framework for understanding how brain-inspired, self-supervised learning of world models facilitates the acquisition of reusable abstract knowledge.

</details>


### [360] [Diversified Residual Symbolic Regression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15809)
*Koki Ikeda,Masahiro Nomura,Ryoki Hamano*

Main category: cs.NE

TL;DR: The paper introduces Diversified Residual Symbolic Regression (DRSR), a method designed to handle outliers in symbolic regression by generating multiple mathematical expressions with diverse residual patterns to aid post-search selection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current symbolic regression methods struggle with outliers, which obscure meaningful regularities, and existing approaches often overlook the role of user input and domain knowledge in handling these irregular observations.

Method: DRSR applies the Quality-Diversity paradigm to generate multiple candidate expressions with varying residual patterns, allowing users to select results aligned with their expertise.

Result: DRSR produces diverse expressions on both synthetic and real-world datasets, capturing multiple relationships while maintaining high predictive accuracy.

Conclusion: DRSR enhances symbolic regression by introducing diversity in residual patterns, supporting user-driven selection and improving interpretability for datasets with outliers.

Abstract: Symbolic regression (SR) aims to discover explicit mathematical expressions that explain observed data and is widely used in domains where interpretability is essential. Because interpretability requires expressions to reflect meaningful regularities, SR is sensitive to observations that deviate from the dominant relationship. Such irregular observations, or outliers, are common in real-world data and can hinder SR from identifying underlying regularities. Robust regression mitigates this by downweighting observations with large residuals. However, deciding which observations should be treated as outliers is often ambiguous and depends on user interpretation and domain knowledge, a perspective largely overlooked in existing SR studies. This motivates approaches that present multiple candidate expressions, allowing users to examine different residual patterns and choose expressions consistent with their expertise. We propose diversified residual symbolic regression (DRSR), which achieves high predictive accuracy while promoting diversity with respect to residual patterns based on the Quality-Diversity paradigm. DRSR collects multiple expressions that fit the data well but differ in how residuals are distributed, enabling post-search selection aligned with domain knowledge. On a synthetic mixture dataset, DRSR produces more diverse expressions than conventional SR while capturing multiple underlying relationships. On a real-world astronomical dataset, DRSR discovers multiple expressions consistent with known physical relationships.

</details>


### [361] [Scalable neuromorphic computing from autonomous spiking dynamics in a clockless reconfigurable chip](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16114)
*Eric Oliveira Gomes,Damien Rontani*

Main category: cs.NE

TL;DR: This paper introduces a scalable neuromorphic architecture utilizing asynchronous digital circuits for spiking dynamics, implemented on FPGAs, achieving power-efficient performance in audio classification tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to create energy-efficient neuromorphic systems using widely available digital hardware, bridging the gap between traditional digital implementations and specialized analog neuromorphic designs.

Method: The method involves implementing networks of Boolean spiking neurons with configurable synaptic weights on FPGAs, coupled with a processing pipeline for spike-encoded data.

Result: The system demonstrated competitive performance in audio classification tasks with reduced power consumption compared to traditional implementations.

Conclusion: Clockless digital hardware is validated as a viable platform for neuromorphic computing, showing potential for reconfigurable chips to become energy-efficient quasi-analog processors.

Abstract: We propose a scalable neuromorphic architecture based on spiking dynamics emerging from the autonomous time-continuous evolution of clockless (asynchronous) digital circuits. Implemented on commercially available field-programmable gate arrays (FPGAs), our system implements networks of interacting Boolean spiking neurons with configurable excitatory and inhibitory synaptic weights. A complete processing pipeline enables efficient handling of spike-encoded data for solving machine-learning tasks. We demonstrate competitive performance for an audio classification task with spike-based encoding and high-speed processing. Power consumption is significantly lower than traditional digital implementations; this makes our approach an efficient alternative that bridges the gap to dedicated analog neuromorphic systems without the need for specialized hardware design. More generally, our approach establishes clockless digital hardware as a viable platform for neuromorphic computing. It paves the way for reconfigurable chips to be turned into energy-efficient quasi-analog neuromorphic processors.

</details>


<div id='cs.PF'></div>

# cs.PF [[Back]](#toc)

### [362] [Heuristic-Based Merging of HPC Traces to Extend Hardware Counter Coverage](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15832)
*Júlia Orteu Aubach,Fabio Banchelli,Marc Clascà Ramírez,Marta Garcia-Gasulla*

Main category: cs.PF

TL;DR: The paper proposes a method to merge execution traces from multiple HPC runs to create a unified dataset for performance prediction using Machine Learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to overcome limitations in performance modeling caused by restrictions on the number of hardware counters collectable simultaneously.

Method: The method uses a heuristic-based approach to merge execution traces by matching computation bursts across runs based on their MPI structure, timing, and communication patterns.

Result: The methodology was validated on the MareNostrum5 machine, showing that merged counters can maintain acceptable accuracy for applications and support ML models with richer feature spaces.

Conclusion: The proposed method allows the usage of a broader set of hardware features for HPC performance prediction and analysis without needing prior counter selection or multiplexing.

Abstract: This work extends a framework for predicting the performance of High-Performance Computing (HPC) workloads using Machine Learning (ML). A common limitation in performance modeling is the restricted number of hardware counters that can be collected simultaneously. To address this, we propose a heuristic-based methodology to merge execution traces from multiple runs, each instrumented with a different set of hardware counters. Our approach matches computation bursts across executions by analyzing MPI structure, timing, and communication patterns. This process enables the construction of a unified dataset that includes a wider set of hardware features without relying on multiplexing. The output is a new synthetic trace with all merged counters, which can be used both for HPC performance prediction and for conventional performance analysis. The methodology has been validated on MareNostrum5 machine with a range of kernels and real applications. Results show that the merged counters maintain acceptable accuracy depending on the application, and can be directly used to train ML models on a richer feature space without prior counter selection.

</details>


<div id='cs.PL'></div>

# cs.PL [[Back]](#toc)

### [363] [Polymorphic Bottom-Up Weighted Relational Programming](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15406)
*Dmitri Volkov*

Main category: cs.PL

TL;DR: The paper introduces a method for implementing polymorphism in bottom-up relational programming without monomorphization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations of monomorphization in relational languages and enable polymorphism efficiently.

Method: Developed semiringKanren, extended it for polymorphism, and proposed a compilation method using equality patterns and sufficient instances of relations.

Result: The method was proven correct, showcasing its efficacy in enabling polymorphic programs for semiringKanren.

Conclusion: The research advances bottom-up relational programming with polymorphism, paving the way for further exploration in this field.

Abstract: This work presents a new approach for implementing polymorphism for bottom-up relational languages, without monomorphization. We begin by introducing semiringKanren, a bottom-up weighted relational programming language. We extend this base language to support polymorphism. We describe a new method to compile polymorphic semiringKanren programs into non-polymorphic ones, based on equality patterns and large-enough instances of polymorphic relations. We prove the correctness of this method. Finally, we consider existing work and suggest directions for future research.

</details>


### [364] [Caesar: A Deductive Verifier for Probabilistic Programs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15827)
*Philipp Schröer,Kevin Batz,Umut Yiğit Dural,Darion Haase,Benjamin Lucien Kaminski,Joost-Pieter Katoen,Christoph Matheja*

Main category: cs.PL

TL;DR: Caesar is a verification tool for probabilistic programs, leveraging HeyVL and HeyLo logic, Z3 SMT solver, and probabilistic model checking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To create a powerful, flexible deductive verification tool for probabilistic programs with easily integratable proof rules.

Method: Designing HeyVL as an intermediate language to translate programs and specifications into verification conditions for validation by Z3 SMT solver and probabilistic model checking.

Result: Five years of development resulted in enhanced features including more proof rules, a model-checking backend, and improved diagnostics.

Conclusion: The advancements in Caesar provide a robust and flexible framework for verifying probabilistic programs.

Abstract: Caesar is a deductive verifier for probabilistic programs. At its core lies HeyVL, a quantitative intermediate verification language based on the real-valued logic HeyLo. HeyVL allows users to express a probabilistic program, its specifications, and proof rules in a programming-language style, so that new proof rules can be easily integrated into the verifier. Caesar translates HeyVL programs into verification conditions, which are then checked using the Z3 SMT solver. It also includes a backend based on probabilistic model checking for a subset of HeyVL. We report on the results of five years of development of Caesar, highlighting its main features and architecture. In particular, we describe recent improvements such as additional proof rules, a model-checking backend, and better diagnostics.

</details>


<div id='cs.RO'></div>

# cs.RO [[Back]](#toc)

### [365] [PhysBrain 1.0 Technical Report](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15298)
*Shijie Lian,Bin Yu,Xiaopeng Lin,Changti Wu,Hang Yuan,Xiaolin Hu,Zhaolong Shen,Yuzhuo Miao,Haishan Liu,Yuxuan Tian,Yukun Shi,Cong Huang,Kai Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PhysBrain 1.0 converts large-scale human egocentric video into structured physical commonsense supervision to improve robot learning and achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) results across multiple benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limited scope of using solely robot trajectories for learning broad physical understanding by leveraging human egocentric videos for structured physical commonsense knowledge.

Method: The study develops a data engine to extract physical commonsense (scene elements, spatial dynamics, and actions) from human egocentric videos and adapt it to train PhysBrain vision-language-action models. This knowledge is further transferred to robot policies via capability-preserving and language-sensitive adaptation.

Result: PhysBrain 1.0 demonstrates state-of-the-art performance across multimodal question-answer benchmarks and embodied control benchmarks, with notable out-of-domain success.

Conclusion: Scaling physical commonsense from human interaction videos provides an effective pathway from multimodal understanding to improving robot actions.

Abstract: Vision-language-action models have advanced rapidly, but robot trajectories alone provide limited coverage for learning broad physical understanding. PhysBrain 1.0 studies a complementary route: converting large-scale human egocentric video into structured physical commonsense supervision before robot adaptation. Our data engine extracts scene elements, spatial dynamics, action execution, and depth-aware relations, then turns them into question-answer supervision for training PhysBrain VLMs. The resulting physical priors are further transferred to VLA policies through a capability-preserving and language-sensitive adaptation design. Across multimodal QA benchmarks and embodied control benchmarks, including ERQA, PhysBench, SimplerEnv-WidowX, LIBERO, and RoboCasa, PhysBrain 1.0 achieves SOTA results and shows especially strong out-of-domain performance on SimplerEnv. These results suggest that scaling physical commonsense from human interaction video can provide an effective bridge from multimodal understanding to robot action.

</details>


### [366] [HoloMotion-1 Technical Report](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15336)
*Maiyue Chen,Kaihui Wang,Bo Zhang,Xihan Ma,Zhiyuan Yang,Yi Ren,Qijun Huang,Zihao Zhu,Yucheng Wang,Zhizhong Su*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces HoloMotion-1, a humanoid model for zero-shot motion tracking trained on a diverse motion dataset and validated with strong results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to overcome the limitations of traditional motion-capture (MoCap)-only training by broadening the diversity, fidelity, and adaptability of motion data.

Method: HoloMotion-1 is trained on a hybrid motion dataset (video-reconstructed and curated data) using a Mixture-of-Experts Transformer with a sequence-level training strategy for real-time motion tracking.

Result: Experiments show robust generalization across motion types, higher tracking accuracy compared to prior methods, and successful transfer to a humanoid robot without fine-tuning.

Conclusion: HoloMotion-1 demonstrates the feasibility of using diverse motion data for zero-shot motion tracking, offering significant improvements over MoCap-only approaches.

Abstract: In this report, we present HoloMotion-1, a humanoid motion foundation model for zero-shot whole-body motion tracking. A key innovation of HoloMotion-1 is to scale control-policy training with a large-scale hybrid motion corpus, where video-reconstructed motions from in-the-wild videos provide the dominant source of motion diversity, while curated motion-capture and in-house motion data provide higher-fidelity supervision and deployment-oriented coverage. This data regime enables HoloMotion-1 to move beyond conventional MoCap-only training and exposes the policy to substantially broader behaviors, capture conditions, and motion styles.
  Learning from such heterogeneous data introduces new challenges, including reconstruction noise, source-domain mismatch, uneven motion quality, and the need for temporal modeling under large behavioral variation. To address these challenges, HoloMotion-1 integrates large-capacity temporal modeling, a sparsely activated Mixture-of-Experts Transformer with KV-cache inference for real-time control, and a sequence-level training strategy that improves learning efficiency on extended motion sequences. Extensive experiments on multiple unseen motion benchmarks show that HoloMotion-1 generalizes robustly across diverse motion types and capture conditions, significantly improves tracking accuracy over prior methods, and transfers directly to a real humanoid robot without task-specific fine-tuning.

</details>


### [367] [Diffusion Policy for Coordinated Control of a Nonholonomic Mobile Base and Dual Arms in Door Opening and Passing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15352)
*Shangqun Yu,Matthew En,Daniel Wu,Sangjun Park,Ziyi Zhou,Seyed Fakoorian,Donghyun Kim*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper addresses the robotic challenge of opening heavy, self-closing pull doors using a novel diffusion-based visuomotor control policy that enables end-to-end learning of manipulation-locomotion tasks with high success rates and robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Opening heavy pull doors involves multiple stages and dexterous movements, which is a long-standing challenge in robotics. Traditional state machine approaches are not robust enough, and there is a need for scalable solutions using advanced imitation learning techniques.

Method: The authors propose using a diffusion-based visuomotor control policy to end-to-end learn the sequence of actions required for complex manipulation and locomotion tasks, such as opening and traversing damped pull doors.

Result: The proposed policy achieves a high success rate in opening and passing through pull doors, demonstrating strong robustness to external disturbances, which traditional methods struggle to achieve.

Conclusion: A single end-to-end diffusion-based visuomotor policy can perform complex, tightly coordinated tasks like opening and traversing doors, offering significant advancements over traditional state machine methods in terms of scalability and robustness.

Abstract: Opening heavy, self closing doors, especially those that require pulling remains a long standing challenge in robotics. Humans naturally employ both arms in a dexterous manner, rotating the handle, widening the gap, holding the door, switching arms when needed, and moving through while maintaining clearance. To replicate such behaviors, a robot must perform a long sequence of motions spanning multiple stages and interactions with different parts of the door. Traditional approaches rely on state machines that transition between manually defined stages (e.g., pulling after the knob is rotated, passing after the gap is sufficiently wide). While intuitive, these methods lack robustness, as hand crafted trajectories fail to generalize to the diversity of real world conditions without extensive engineering effort. Recent advances in imitation learning offer a scalable alternative, yet no existing visual action model has demonstrated simultaneous coordination of a nonholonomic base and dual arms for the complete door opening and passing task. In this paper, we tackle this complex, highly constrained problem using a diffusion based visuomotor control policy. Our results demonstrate that a single end to end policy can be learned to execute long horizon tasks requiring tight coordination between manipulation and locomotion. The resulting policy not only achieves a high success rate in opening and traversing damped pull doors but also demonstrates strong robustness to external disturbances capabilities that are difficult to realize with traditional methods.

</details>


### [368] [Where to Perch in a Tree: Vision-Guidance for Tree-Grasping Drones](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15430)
*Alex Dunnett,Leonie Bottomley,Mirko Kovac,Basaran Bahadir Kocer*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a vision-based method enabling drones to locate optimal perch locations on trees by evaluating branch factors like width, angle, and curvature, achieving 76% success on suitable targets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper is motivated by the need for improved autonomous tree-perching drones that require precise assessment of tree branches to ensure safe and effective landing.

Method: Using machine learning, image segmentation, and binary image morphology, the method evaluates tree branches' suitability for perching, considering parameters such as branch width, slope, and curvature.

Result: The method was tested with over 10,000 urban tree images in varying climates and successfully identified feasible perching targets in 76% of the cases.

Conclusion: The approach lays the groundwork for further development by integrating additional sensing techniques, aiming for a generalized method to optimize tree-perching for drones.

Abstract: This study demonstrates a method to locate an ideal perch location on a tree for vision-guided autonomous tree-perching drones. Various image processing algorithms, including those used for machine learning, image segmentation and binary image morphology, are implemented to assess the shape and structure of a tree. Rather than identifying the closest available branch, this study builds on vision methods by evaluating the potential of each branch, determining its suitability for perching based on factors such as branch width, slope (angle to the horizontal) and curvature. For a given tree-perching drone and a dataset of more than 10,000 urban tree images taken from February to October in a subtropical and temperate monsoon climate, the proposed method successfully produces a result for 76% of feasible targets. A feasible target defined as a tree where the branch diameters are sufficiently thick and where the available perching space is at least equal to the width of a tendon-driven grasping claw. These successful preliminary results create a foundation from which a number of identified improvements and additional features can be developed to create a generalised method; this will involve the incorporation of supplementary data from depth perception and attitude sensors to enhance the branch assessment.

</details>


### [369] [Residual Reinforcement Learning for Robot Teleoperation under Stochastic Delays](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15480)
*Kaize Deng,Zewen Yang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper addresses issues caused by stochastic communication delays in teleoperation by proposing a hybrid control framework combining LSTM-based state estimation and a residual RL torque compensation policy to enhance control performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the problem of signal discontinuity and chattering in conventional RL approaches caused by stochastic delays in teleoperation.

Method: The proposed method integrates a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)-based state estimator for reconstructing continuous state estimates and a residual reinforcement learning (RL) policy for achieving smooth torque compensation.

Result: Experimental findings on Franka Panda robots show that their approach outperforms existing methods, ensuring stability and robustness under high-variance delays.

Conclusion: The delay-resilient RL framework is effective for teleoperation systems, offering improved stability and control despite challenges from stochastic communication delays.

Abstract: Stochastic communication delays in teleoperation introduce signal discontinuities that undermine control stability and degrade control performance. Consequently, the conventional reinforcement learning (RL) methods struggle with the delayed observations due to the delay-induced observations, leading to high-frequency chattering. To address this, we propose a hybrid control framework, delay-resilient RL, integrating a state estimator utilizing Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) with a residual RL policy, which is resilient to stochastic delays. The LSTM reconstructs smooth, continuous state estimates from delayed observations, enabling the RL agent to learn a residual torque compensation policy that balances tracking accuracy with velocity smoothness. Experimental validation on Franka Panda robots demonstrates that our approach significantly outperforms the state-of-the-art baselines, ensuring robust and stable teleoperation even under high-variance stochastic delays.

</details>


### [370] [Hybrid LLM-based Intelligent Framework for Robot Task Scheduling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15486)
*Swayamjit Saha,Subhabrata Das,Haonan Duan,Xiao-Yang Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The study develops a framework using Large Language Models (LLMs) like GPT-4 and others to optimize task scheduling for construction robots, improving efficiency and adaptability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance task scheduling for construction robots by leveraging the intelligence of LLMs, aiming for better time efficiency and resource optimization.

Method: The method involves employing LLMs (a generator and a supervisor) to analyze data such as agent action abilities and task goals. A natural language interface is used for real-time adaptability and communication.

Result: The framework has been tested on a basic scenario and demonstrated improved scheduling efficacy through provided metric scores.

Conclusion: LLMs significantly improve operational tasks like task scheduling in construction robots, showcasing potential for widespread implementation in this field.

Abstract: This study introduces intelligent frameworks that use Large Language Models (LLMs) to improve task scheduling for construction robots. The LLM is fed with key data about the desired task, such as agent action abilities, and the desired end goal to be achieved. A well-balanced allocation strategy is developed, optimizing both time efficiency and resource utilization. Our system utilizes a Natural Language Processing interface to streamline communication with construction professionals and adapt in real-time to unexpected site conditions. We concurrently use two LLM agents, specifically generator (GPT-4) and supervisor (Gemma 3/Llama 4/Mistral 7b) LLM agents to provide a more precise task schedule. We evaluate the proposed methodology using a straightforward scenario and provide metric scores to prove the efficacy of the frameworks. Our results highlight that the implementation of LLMs is crucial in construction operational tasks including robots.

</details>


### [371] [FLASH: Efficient Visuomotor Policy via Sparse Sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15492)
*Jiaqi Bai,Jindou Jia,Yuxuan Hu,Gen Li,Xiangyu Chen,Tuo An,Kuangji Zuo,Jianfei Yang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The study introduces FLASH Policy, a generative visuomotor policy that reduces inference latency, enabling real-time robotic control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the high inference latency of existing generative visuomotor policies like diffusion models, which hinder their applicability in real-time robotic controls.

Method: Proposes FLASH Policy, which employs Legendre polynomial trajectory representations and sparse history-based flow matching to achieve efficient, continuous, and fast policy generation for visuomotor tasks.

Result: FLASH outperforms others in success rates (≥92%), accelerates training (up to 4×), reduces inference time (31.40 ms), achieves faster convergence than ACT, and reduces controller tracking error by 5× to 7×.

Conclusion: FLASH overcomes the limitations of iterative generative models for visuomotor control and proves highly efficient and accurate across both simulated and real-world tasks.

Abstract: Generative models such as diffusion and flow matching have become dominant paradigms for visuomotor policy learning, yet their reliance on iterative denoising incurs high inference latency incompatible with real-time robotic control. We present Fast Legendre-polynomial Action policy via Sparse History-anchored flow (FLASH Policy), which replaces discrete action-chunk generation with continuous Legendre polynomial trajectory representation. Specifically, by fitting expert demonstrations under sparse temporal sampling, FLASH enables a single inference to cover a significantly extended action horizon. To further accelerate generation, FLASH initiates the flow matching process from history polynomial coefficients rather than uninformative Gaussian noise, shortening the transport distance and enabling accurate single-step inference. Moreover, analytic polynomial differentiation directly provides desired velocity feed-forward signals to the torque controller without numerical approximation. Extensive experiments on five simulated and two real-world manipulation tasks demonstrate that FLASH achieves state-of-the-art success rates ($\ge 92\%$ across all tasks), a per-episode inference time of $31.40\,ms$ (up to $175\times$ faster than diffusion policies and $18\times$ faster than prior flow matching policies), up to $4\times$ faster training convergence than ACT, and $5\times$ to $7\times$ reduction in controller tracking error compared to discrete-action baselines.

</details>


### [372] [LAPS: Improving Incremental LiDAR Mapping using Active Pooling and Sampling for Neural Distance Fields](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15496)
*Dongjae Lee,Wooseong Yang,Yifu Tao,Maurice Fallon,Ayoung Kim*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes LAPS, a framework to improve replay mechanisms in neural distance fields for incremental LiDAR mapping, addressing memory efficiency and under-trained regions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addressing the challenges of catastrophic forgetting in online optimization of neural distance fields for LiDAR mapping, particularly memory waste with redundant observations and under-trained regions.

Method: LAPS uses reliability-based active pooling for retaining reliable historical samples under memory constraints and uncertainty-guided active sampling to focus on under-constrained areas.

Result: Experiments on synthetic and real-world benchmarks demonstrate that LAPS enhances reconstruction completeness and achieves notable improvements in recall and F1-score over existing methods like PIN-SLAM.

Conclusion: LAPS effectively addresses memory and optimization challenges in replay-based training for neural distance fields, ensuring superior results in incremental LiDAR mapping.

Abstract: Neural distance fields offer a compact and continuous representation of 3D geometry, making them attractive for incremental LiDAR mapping. However, their online optimization is vulnerable to catastrophic forgetting, where new observations can degrade previously reconstructed geometry. Replay-based training is commonly used to address this issue, but existing methods typically rely on passive replay buffers and uniform sampling, which can waste memory on redundant observations and under-train poorly constrained regions. We propose LAPS, a replay management framework for incremental neural mapping that improves both replay retention and replay allocation during online updates. LAPS combines reliability-based active pooling to retain reliable historical samples under limited memory with uncertainty-guided active sampling to focus optimization on under-constrained regions. Experiments on synthetic and real-world benchmarks show that LAPS consistently improves reconstruction completeness while maintaining competitive geometric accuracy. On Oxford Spires, it improves recall by 4.66 pp and F1-score by 3.79 pp over PIN-SLAM on the Blenheim Palace 05 sequence. We release our open source implementation at: https://github.com/dongjae0107/LAPS.

</details>


### [373] [A QUBO Formulation Framework for Kinematic Structure-Based Robot Design Optimization: A Robotic Hand Case Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15510)
*HyoJae Kang,Yeong Jae Park,Jeongdo Ahn,Dongil Park*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper proposes a quadratic unconstrained binary optimization (QUBO) framework for robot design, demonstrated on a robotic hand. It bridges classical and quantum computation for combinatorial problems and validates feasibility with simulated and quantum annealing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To create a generalized framework that bridges kinematic structure-based robot design optimization with quantum annealing, addressing combinatorial design challenges.

Method: The framework evaluates kinematic metrics using classical computation and formulates a quadratic model compatible with quantum annealing. A robotic hand example (27 variables) is analyzed using simulated annealing and quantum annealing.

Result: The study successfully formulates feasible robotic hand designs satisfying one-hot and pairwise constraints. Quantum annealing output shows improved objective values with increased reads, confirming hardware compatibility.

Conclusion: The framework provides a generalized method to convert kinematic structure-based robot design problems into combinatorial optimization tasks, paving the way for further quantum computation applications in robotic designs.

Abstract: This paper presents a quadratic unconstrained binary optimization-based formulation framework for robot design optimization using kinematic structure-level evaluation metrics. In the proposed framework, classical computation is used to evaluate design-dependent metrics while the resulting combinatorial selection problem is formulated in a structure compatible with quantum annealing-based optimization. A robotic hand is adopted as a representative case study, as its performance is determined by both the individual kinematic characteristics of each finger and interaction terms. The proposed formulation incorporates individual design rewards, overlap workspace interactions, one-hot constraint, and structural dependency penalties into a unified quadratic model. A 27-variable robotic hand design problem is constructed, and simulated annealing is used as a classical baseline to verify the feasibility of the formulation. Quantum annealing is further performed to examine the applicability of the proposed formulation to annealing-based hardware execution. The results show that feasible design combinations satisfying both one-hot selection and pairwise constraints can be obtained, with the observed objective-value range becoming narrower as the number of reads increases. In addition, the formulation process is discussed for other robotic systems. The proposed framework provides a generalized approach for transforming kinematic structure-based robot design problems into combinatorial optimization problems.

</details>


### [374] [Terrain Consistent Reference-Guided RL for Humanoid Navigation Autonomy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15517)
*William D. Compton,Zachary Olkin,Aaron D. Ames*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces a method for training locomotion policies in humanoid robots by using terrain-aware reference trajectories, tested successfully on rough outdoor terrain and stairs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable humanoid robots to handle complex terrains while maintaining compatibility with standard navigation autonomy tools.

Method: The method trains reference-guided reinforcement learning locomotion policies, using SE(2)-controllable reference trajectories adapted to terrain geometry during training.

Result: The approach improves reference tracking in simulation and is demonstrated in hardware for long-horizon autonomous navigation (over 70m) on rough terrain and stairs.

Conclusion: Terrain-conditioned reference trajectories can significantly enhance humanoid robot navigation, enabling robust performance in complex real-world environments.

Abstract: We present a method for training reference-guided, perceptive reinforcement learning locomotion policies for humanoid robots in which reference trajectories are modulated in training to be consistent with terrain geometry. Aiming to deploy our method with standard navigation autonomy infrastructure, we synthesize SE(2)-controllable reference trajectories inside the RL training loop, projecting desired footsteps onto valid footholds and adjusting swing-foot and center-of-mass trajectories to match the terrain. The resulting policy exposes a clean SE(2) velocity interface compatible with standard navigation planners. In simulation, environmentally-conditioned references significantly improve reference tracking performance compared to environment agnostic references. On hardware, we integrate the policy with an MPC + control barrier function planner and demonstrate long-horizon (>70m) closed-loop autonomous navigation on the Unitree G1 through outdoor environments containing rough terrain and consecutive flights of stairs, with all sensing and computation onboard.

</details>


### [375] [Task-Semantic Graph-Driven Distributed Agent Networking for Underwater Target Tracking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15528)
*Shengchao Zhu,Guangjie Han,Chuan Lin,Yu He*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces an open-source MARL-AUV platform combining a MARL training framework with a six-degree-of-freedom underwater AUV swarm simulator, proposing STG-MAPPO for enhanced cooperation in target tracking tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address the lack of a unified open-source platform to evaluate MARL algorithms in AUV swarm applications, and improve upon existing limitations in policy representation for underwater tracking tasks.

Method: The developed platform integrates DI-engine with a six-degree-of-freedom simulator, offering a unified protocol for evaluating RL and MARL algorithms. The authors also propose STG-MAPPO, which uses semantic task graphs to improve decision-making and action abstraction.

Result: The developed platform integrates realistic underwater AUV swarm tasks with public MARL frameworks, and STG-MAPPO demonstrates improved tracking and coordination benefits compared to baseline methods.

Conclusion: The study contributes by offering the first open-source platform for MARL in AUV swarms and introduces STG-MAPPO to enhance decentralized actor decisions and underwater tracking efficiency.

Abstract: Autonomous underwater vehicle (AUV) swarms are emerging as intelligent underwater networks, where each node must sense, communicate, process local data, and make decisions under severe acoustic constraints. Persistent underwater target tracking is a typical task with moving targets, changing communication topology, intermittent acoustic links, and limited observation for each AUV. Multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) is a natural candidate for distributed tracking, yet existing studies still lack a unified open-source platform for evaluating different MARL algorithms under six-degree-of-freedom AUV dynamics. In addition, policies trained with raw geometric states and low-level force actions often struggle to represent task phases, observation reliability, link quality, and local cooperation roles. This paper addresses these issues by developing an open-source MARL-AUV platform that integrates DI-engine with a six-degree-of-freedom underwater AUV target-tracking simulator. To the best of our knowledge, it is the first open platform that connects a public MARL training framework with physically modeled AUV swarm-based tasks, and provides a unified experimental protocol for fair training, testing, and comparison of representative RL and MARL algorithms. Based on this platform, we propose STG-MAPPO, a Semantic Task Graph-enhanced variant of Multi-Agent Proximal Policy Optimization. STG-MAPPO builds semantic policy inputs from tracking diagnostics, task phases, observation confidence, link availability, neighbor tracking quality, and local role advantage. A compact semantic task graph links communication-constrained network states to decentralized actor decisions, and a velocity-level action abstraction maps high-level cooperative decisions to executable six-degree-offreedom AUV control inputs.The code is available at https://github.com/dasjsaj/MARL-AUV.

</details>


### [376] [SkiP: When to Skip and When to Refine for Efficient Robot Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15536)
*Mingtong Dai,Guanqi Peng,Yongjie Bai,Feng Yan,Chunjie Chen,Lingbo Liu,Liang Lin,Xinyu Wu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces Skip Policy (SkiP), an imitation learning method that skips redundant steps in manipulation tasks while focusing on key action points, improving efficiency and success rates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Imitation learning policies traditionally predict actions at every step, even though many steps involve low-relevance tasks; this results in inefficiency. This paper aims to optimize this process by focusing on key action points for better task performance and efficiency.

Method: The authors propose SkiP, which relabels actions in skip segments to jump directly to key action segments. A task-agnostic method called Motion Spectrum Keying (MSK) is used to identify key and skip segments from action data, enabling automatic task segmentation.

Result: Experiments on 72 simulated tasks and three real-robot tasks show that SkiP reduces redundant steps by 15–40% while maintaining or improving success rates across various policy backbones.

Conclusion: SkiP eliminates inefficiencies in imitation learning by dynamically leaping over redundant steps and intensifying focus on essential ones. This innovation enhances task execution efficiency and does not require additional hierarchical or planner structures.

Abstract: Previous imitation learning policies predict future actions at every control step, whether in smooth motion phases or precise, contact-rich operation phases. This uniform treatment is wasteful: most steps in a manipulation trajectory traverse free space and carry little task-relevant information, while a small fraction of \emph{key} steps around contacts, grasps, and alignment demand dense, high-resolution prediction. We propose a novel \emph{action relabeling} mechanism: at each timestep in a skip segment, we replace the behavior cloning target with the action at the entrance of the next key segment, enabling the policy to leap over redundant steps in a single decision. The resulting \textbf{Skip Policy (SkiP)} dynamically leaps over skip segments and intensively refines actions in key segments, within a single unified network requiring no learned skip planner or hierarchical structure. To automatically partition demonstrations into key and skip segments without manual annotation, we introduce \emph{Motion Spectrum Keying} (MSK), a fast, task-agnostic procedure that detects local motion complexity from action signals. Extensive experiments across 72 simulated manipulation tasks and three real-robot tasks show that SkiP reduces executed steps by $15$--$40\%$ while matching or improving success rates across various policy backbones. Project page: \texttt{https://pgq18.github.io/SkiP-page/}.

</details>


### [377] [KaRMA: A Kinematic Metric for Fine Manipulation Ability in Robotic Hands](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15548)
*Martin Peticco,Pulkit Agrawal*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces a new kinematic-only metric called KaRMA to measure robotic hand's dexterity under in-hand manipulation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional robotic hand metrics inadequately capture dexterity as defined by the ability to manipulate an object's pose within the hand.

Method: Developed KaRMA, which evaluates in-hand object manipulation by modeling rolling motions while respecting constraints and quantifies reachable poses using breadth-first search over translation and rotation primitives.

Result: KaRMA assesses dexterity using three scores—KaRMA-T, KaRMA-R, KaRMA-S—and successfully differentiates robotic hand performance where static metrics fail.

Conclusion: KaRMA provides a robust dexterity metric for robotic hands, surpassing the insights offered by traditional static measures and Jacobian-based metrics.

Abstract: Traditional robotic hand metrics focus on static properties such as workspace, manipulability, and grasp stability. However, these metrics do not directly measure dexterity under the standard definition in robotic manipulation: the ability to continuously change an object's pose within the hand while maintaining contact from an initial grasp. We introduce Kinematic Rolling Manipulation Ability (KaRMA), a kinematic-only metric for fine manipulation that quantifies reachable in-hand translation and reorientation of a spherical test object within a two-finger precision pinch through feasible rolling motions. KaRMA enforces joint limits, collision constraints, rolling contact, and antipodal force feasibility, then investigates reachable in-hand object poses via breadth-first search over translation and rotation primitives. KaRMA reports three scores: translational coverage (KaRMA-T), rotational coverage (KaRMA-R), and sensitivity to the initial grasp (KaRMA-S). We evaluate KaRMA on 16 widely used robotic hands and compare against static baselines, showing that KaRMA separates hands that rank identically under static proxies, reveals translation-rotation tradeoffs invisible to existing baselines, and is qualitatively consistent with selected published task benchmarks where Jacobian-based metrics can be misleading.

</details>


### [378] [NavRL++: A System-Level Framework for Improving Sim-to-Real Transfer in Reinforcement Learning-Based Robot Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15559)
*Zhefan Xu,Hanyu Jin,Kenji Shimada*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper focuses on enhancing autonomous navigation through reinforcement learning by addressing sim-to-real transfer challenges and proposes new training strategies and Transformer-based policy architectures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of analysis of sim-to-real transfer and insights into training strategies in reinforcement learning-based navigation methods.

Method: Introduced a complete training and deployment pipeline alongside a perturbation-aware fine-tuning strategy and a Transformer-based temporal reasoning policy for navigation control.

Result: Achieved improved robustness in sim-to-real settings, outperforming learning-based baselines and matching optimization-based planners in static environments.

Conclusion: The methods and strategies proposed enable effective zero-shot sim-to-real deployment across various robotic platforms and navigation tasks.

Abstract: Recent years have witnessed significant progress in autonomous navigation using reinforcement learning. However, existing approaches largely emphasize reinforcement learning framework design, such as input representations, action spaces, and reward functions, while providing limited analysis of sim-to-real transfer and insufficient insight into how training strategies affect real-world deployment performance. To bridge this gap, we not only introduce an effective RL framework but also present a complete training and deployment pipeline, along with a systematic empirical study that disentangles the key factors affecting sim-to-real transfer in reinforcement learning-based navigation, including sensor noise, perception failures, system latency, and control response. Building on insights from this analysis, we introduce perturbation-aware fine-tuning, a post-training adaptation strategy that improves transfer robustness by explicitly accounting for empirically identified domain discrepancies. To further mitigate perception degradation and enhance control smoothness in real-world deployment, we propose a Transformer-based temporal reasoning policy that leverages short-horizon observation for navigation control. We quantitatively evaluate how individual sim-to-real perturbations and training design choices impact navigation performance across environments. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed training strategy and policy architecture outperform learning-based baselines in both static and dynamic environments, while achieving performance comparable to optimization-based planners in static settings. We validate our approach through real-world deployment on multiple robotic platforms, including aerial and legged robots, across navigation-centric tasks such as exploration and inspection, demonstrating zero-shot sim-to-real transfer.

</details>


### [379] [Wind-Aware Optimal Trajectory Planning for Efficient Gliding of Fixed-Wing Aerial Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15619)
*Luca Morando,Nishanth Bobbili,Giuseppe Loianno*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper presents a nonlinear trajectory-planning method for UAV gliders to improve energy management under wind, using Bernstein polynomials and differential flatness for control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enhance UAV gliders’ energy efficiency and operability under wind disturbances and constraints without relying entirely on reactive controllers.

Method: Develop a nonlinear, multi-cost trajectory planner leveraging Bernstein polynomials for smooth trajectories, integrated with air mass estimations through a simulated netto variometer and differential flatness for control.

Result: Validated through CFD simulations and real-world tests, the approach stabilizes key flight characteristics like sink rate and airspeed even under challenging conditions.

Conclusion: Planning-level energy regulation significantly improves UAV glider performance, making hybrid missions more feasible by combining powered and gliding modes.

Abstract: Gliding offers small fixed-wing UAVs extended endurance and silent operation but requires accurate energy management, especially under wind disturbances and obstacle constraints. Traditional Total Energy Control Systems based controllers regulate the trade between potential and kinetic energy reactively, often requiring fine-tuning and trim-conditions knowledge. In this work, we shift the regulation to the planning level and present a nonlinear, multi-cost trajectory planner for small UAV gliders. The method generates $\mathcal{C}^3$ continuous trajectories based on Bernstein polynomials, mapped into control commands through differential flatness, and re-planned online to match experimentally derived sink polar curves. A simulated netto variometer is integrated into the optimization to estimate air mass motion, constraining the glide to energy-balanced states. Consecutive gliding trajectories are linked by cruising segments computed through trajectories initialized on Dubins path-based waypoints, enabling hybrid missions that combine powered and unpowered flight. The approach is validated in CFD simulations and real-world experiments with a fixed-wing platform, showing reliable stabilization of sink rate, airspeed, and glide ratio under wind gusts and in presence of obstacles.

</details>


### [380] [Propagating Unsafe Actions in LLM Controlled Multi-Robot Collaboration via Single Robot Compromise](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15641)
*Zhen Huang,Zhihuang Liu,Weishang Wu,Zhiping Cai*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper explores security vulnerabilities in multi-robot systems controlled by large language models (LLMs), particularly regarding how adversarial instructions propagate through inter-robot communication and compromise safety.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the evolving reliance on LLMs for managing multi-robot collaboration, highlighting critical security vulnerabilities that arise from manipulated instructions, which have not been thoroughly examined in prior work.

Method: The authors propose a novel attack on multi-robot systems where an adversary compromises a single robot, exploiting inter-robot communication to propagate malicious instructions system-wide. They evaluate this attack using three metrics: obedience, infectiousness, and stealthiness.

Result: The experiments reveal high levels of attacker control and rapid spread of malicious instructions: obedience scores reached 1.00, infectiousness 0.90, and stealthiness 0.81, with all robots compromised in as few as three rounds.

Conclusion: Multi-robot systems managed by LLMs face significant security challenges, especially in critical situations where coordination can compromise safety. Effective defenses are urgently needed to mitigate the risks outlined in this research.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used as general planners in embodied intelligence, enabling high level coordination and low level task planning for both single robot and multi-robot collaboration. This increasing reliance on embodied LLM planners also raises critical security concerns, since misaligned or manipulated instructions can be translated into physical actions. Prior work has studied such threats in single robot settings, while security risks in LLM controlled multi-robot collaboration, especially those propagated through inter robot communication, remain largely unexplored. To bridge this gap, we propose a novel attack paradigm for multi-robot system in which the adversary interacts with only a single entry robot.
