We introduce Fortress, a general framework for enhancing model stability and accuracy by identifying and pruning features that contribute to inconsistent prediction scores over time. Fortress leverages historical snapshots temporally partitioned datasets capturing score fluctuations for the same entity across periods and follows a four-step process: (1) collect historical snapshots, (2) identify samples with unstable predictions, (3) isolate and remove instability-inducing features, and (4) retrain models using only stable features. While semantic features from LLMs and BERT-based models improve generalization, they often lack full query or entity coverage. Engagement-based features offer strong predictive power but tend to introduce temporal instability. Fortress mitigates this trade-off by suppressing the volatility of engagement signals while retaining their predictive value leading to more stable and accurate models. We validate Fortress on a query-to-app relevance model in a large-scale app marketplace. Offline experiments demonstrate notable improvements in prediction stability (measured by Coefficient of Variation) and classification performance (measured by PR-AUC).

</details>


### [495] [Differentially Private Motif-Preserving Multi-modal Hashing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15460)
*Zehua Cheng,Wei Dai,Jiahao Sun*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: The paper introduces DMP-MH, a privacy-preserving cross-modal hashing framework that addresses vulnerability issues in semantic similarity graphs by employing privacy-protective graph sanitization and efficient representation learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to address the privacy vulnerabilities in cross-modal hashing methods that rely on semantic similarity graphs, which can expose sensitive behavioral patterns to link reconstruction attacks.

Method: To mitigate privacy concerns, the researchers propose a Sanitize-then-Distill approach called DMP-MH. This includes deterministically clipping node degrees to bound sensitivity, generating a sanitized synthetic graph using Noisy Mirror Descent with Edge Differential Privacy, and training hashing networks with a structural loss to enforce cross-modal alignment.

Result: Experimental results on MIRFlickr-25K and NUS-WIDE benchmarks show that DMP-MH achieves up to 11.4 mAP points improvement over private baselines while retaining up to 92.5% of non-private performance.

Conclusion: The proposed DMP-MH framework effectively balances privacy preservation and performance in cross-modal hashing, demonstrating significant improvements over existing privacy-preserving approaches and retaining most of the non-private model's effectiveness.

Abstract: Cross-modal hashing enables efficient retrieval by encoding images and text into compact binary codes. State-of-the-art methods rely on semantic similarity graphs derived from user interactions for supervision, yet these graphs encode sensitive behavioral patterns vulnerable to link reconstruction attacks. Existing privacy-preserving approaches fail on graph-structured data: Differentially Private SGD destroys relational motifs by treating samples independently, while graph synthesis methods suffer from unbounded local sensitivity in scale-free networks, hub nodes cause single-edge modifications to alter triangle counts by $\mathcal{O}(N)$, necessitating prohibitive noise injection. We term this phenomenon Hubness Explosion. We propose DMP-MH, a Sanitize-then-Distill framework that decouples privacy from representation learning. Our approach first bounds sensitivity by deterministically clipping node degrees, capping the $L_2$-sensitivity of triangle motifs independently of dataset size. A sanitized synthetic graph is then generated via Noisy Mirror Descent under $(ε,δ)$-Edge Differential Privacy. Finally, dual-stream hashing networks distill this topology using a holistic structural loss that enforces cross-modal alignment. Evaluated on MIRFlickr-25K and NUS-WIDE under a strict inductive protocol, DMP-MH outperforms private baselines by up to 11.4 mAP points while retaining up to 92.5% of non-private performance.

</details>


### [496] [Generative Long-term User Interest Modeling for Click-Through Rate Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15905)
*Jiangli Shao,Kaifu Zheng,Hao Fang,Huimu Ye,Zhiwei Liu,Bo Zhang,Shu Han,Xingxing Wang*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: The paper introduces GenLI, a generative model for CTR prediction in advertising and recommendation systems, which enhances user interest modeling by avoiding limitations of traditional retrieval methods and improves efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current CTR prediction frameworks often miss latent user interests and rely on time-consuming matching-based retrieval methods, which become inefficient as user behavior history grows.

Method: GenLI features three core modules: Interest Generation Module (IGM) for generating diverse and target-independent interest distributions, Behavior Retrieval Module (BRM) with time-efficient lookup operations, and Interest Fusion Module (IFM) using a gating mechanism to synthesize features.

Result: GenLI improves user interest modeling by providing diverse and complete interest features while achieving a balance between accuracy and efficiency in CTR prediction tasks.

Conclusion: By addressing limitations of traditional two-stage frameworks, GenLI offers a more effective and efficient approach for modeling long-term user interests in CTR prediction systems.

Abstract: Modeling long-term user interests with massive historical user behaviors enhances click-through rate (CTR) prediction performance in advertising and recommendation systems. Typically, a two-stage framework is widely adopted, where a general search unit (GSU) first retrieves top-$k$ relevant behaviors towards the target item, and an exact search unit (ESU) generates interest features via tailored attention. However, current target-centered GSU would ignore other latent user interests, leading to incomplete and biased interest features. Additionally, the matching-based retrieval process in GSUs depends on the pairwise similarity score between target item and each historical behavior, which not only becomes time-consuming for online services as user behaviors continue to grow, but also overlooks the interaction information among user behaviors. To combat these problems, we propose a \textbf{Gen}erative \textbf{L}ong-term user \textbf{I}nterest model named GenLI for CTR prediction. GenLI consists of an interest generation module (IGM), a behavior retrieval module (BRM), and an interest fusion module (IFM). The IGM generates multiple interest distributions to indicate different aspects of real-time user interests, which is target-independent and incorporates interaction information among behaviors, ensuring complete and diverse interest features. The BRM selects related behaviors via a simple lookup operation, reducing the time complexity for weighting each behavior to $O(1)$. Finally, the IFM uses delicate gating mechanisms to generate interest features. Based on the generation process, GenLI improves the diversity of user interests and avoids complex matching-based behavioral retrieval, achieving a better balance between accuracy and efficiency for CTR prediction.

</details>


<div id='cs.SI'></div>

# cs.SI [[Back]](#toc)

### [497] [CitePrism: Human-in-the-Loop AI for Citation Auditing and Editorial Integrity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16000)
*Gowrika Mahesh,Budanur Madappa Darshan Gowda,Kavana Gopladevarahalli Papegowda,Prajwal Basavaraj,Binh Vu,Swati Chandna,Mehrdad Jalali*

Main category: cs.SI

TL;DR: CitePrism is a hybrid decision-support tool for editorial citation auditing, combining advanced technologies with human review to enhance citation quality. Preliminary testing flagged all irrelevant citations but requires broader validation for real-world deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Citation auditing remains a largely manual, fragmented, and challenging task for editors and reviewers, requiring more scalable and effective solutions.

Method: CitePrism employs a combination of large language models (LLMs), semantic similarity embeddings, metadata verification, integrity flags, and human-in-the-loop review to audit citations.

Result: During case-study validation on a single manuscript, the system achieved a moderate agreement (Cohen's kappa = 0.429) with human relevance judgments and flagged irrelevant citations successfully, albeit with some false positives.

Conclusion: CitePrism shows potential in supporting editorial citation audits and quality checks but requires extensive validation across diverse contexts before operational deployment.

Abstract: Editors and reviewers are expected to ensure that manuscripts cite relevant, accurate, current, and ethically appropriate literature, yet manuscript-level citation auditing remains largely manual, fragmented, and difficult to scale. Citation context, metadata quality, self-citation patterns, and bibliographic integrity all affect whether a reference appropriately supports a local claim. We present CitePrism, a transparent hybrid decision-support framework for editorial citation auditing that combines LLM-assisted contextual reasoning, embedding-based semantic similarity, metadata verification, integrity-oriented flags, and human-in-the-loop analyst review. CitePrism extracts citation neighborhoods, enriches reference metadata, computes fused relevance scores, surfaces metadata and self-citation review prompts, and supports configurable threshold-based triage. In a preliminary validation on a single case-study manuscript with 104 references from pavement engineering, agreement with human binary relevance labels reached Cohen's kappa = 0.429. At operating threshold tau = 17, CitePrism flagged all human-labeled irrelevant citations, while also producing false positives requiring analyst review. These results suggest that CitePrism may support conservative editorial screening and citation-quality triage, but they do not establish general editorial performance. CitePrism is intended as pilot-stage decision support, not as an autonomous misconduct detector or automated editorial decision system. Broader validation across manuscripts, domains, annotators, baselines, and deployment settings is required before operational use.

</details>


<div id='quant-ph'></div>

# quant-ph [[Back]](#toc)

### [498] [Quantum mechanics for classical transport equations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15969)
*Christof Wetterich*

Main category: quant-ph

TL;DR: The paper maps classical transport equations with probabilistic initial conditions to quantum systems, demonstrating their parallels with quantum mechanics' key features.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To connect classical probabilistic transport frameworks with the well-known principles and features of quantum mechanics, thereby providing a novel perspective on probabilistic systems.

Method: The authors frame classical probabilistic transport through a discrete probabilistic automata model, encode time-local probability in a classical wave function, and derive its unitary Schrödinger evolution using a complex functional integral.

Result: The study reveals that statistical observables in such frameworks behave like quantum operators, showcasing non-commuting behavior and properties like superposition, interference, and unitary evolution.

Conclusion: Classical probabilistic transport systems can be effectively interpreted through the lens of quantum mechanics' fundamental principles, bridging the understanding of classical and quantum dynamics.

Abstract: Classical transport equations with probabilistic initial conditions can be viewed as quantum systems.
  In a discrete version they are probabilistic automata. The time-local probabilistic information is encoded in a classical wave function.
  Its unitary evolution obeys a Schrödinger equation.
  Statistical observables are represented by operators which do not commute with the ones associated to classical observables.
  Examples are functions of the quantum energy or the quantum angular momentum. They are important conserved quantities.
  We construct a complex functional integral for the quantum system which describes the probabilistic classical transport equation.
  The characteristic features of quantum mechanics, as the superposition of wave functions, interference, the importance of phases,
  non-commuting operators or a unitary time evolution, are realized by probabilistic classical transport equations.

</details>


### [499] [Thermodynamic Networks: Harnessing Non-Equilibrium Steady States for Computation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15985)
*Patryk Lipka-Bartosik,Gianmichele Blasi,Javier Lalueza Puértolas,Géraldine Haack,Martí Perarnau-Llobet,Nicolas Brunner*

Main category: quant-ph

TL;DR: The paper introduces thermodynamic networks as a framework for physics-based computation, leveraging non-equilibrium steady states and Negative Differential Conductance (NDC) for universal function approximation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to explore autonomous and physics-based computational systems by utilizing properties of non-equilibrium steady states to encode solutions, expanding computational capabilities.

Method: The authors model networks as finite-size reservoirs exchanging conserved quantities and identify NDC as a key property for computational flexibility. Training utilizes the system's natural equilibration tendencies, and they demonstrate with quantum dot and enzymatic reaction networks.

Result: The proposed thermodynamic networks achieve high performance in computational tasks such as sine function approximation and MNIST digit classification, demonstrating the potential of the framework.

Conclusion: The study establishes a foundational connection between non-equilibrium steady states, NDC, and computational capabilities, paving the way for versatile, physics-grounded computational platforms.

Abstract: We introduce thermodynamic networks, a general framework for autonomous, physics-based computation using non-equilibrium steady states. These networks are modeled as a collection of finite-size reservoirs that exchange conserved quantities--such as electric charge or molecular number--while relaxing to a non-equilibrium steady state, which encodes the solution of a computational problem. We identify Negative Differential Conductance (NDC) as the critical physical property governing the computational expressivity of the thermodynamic network. While networks lacking NDC are restricted to computing monotonic functions, the presence of NDC enables universal function approximation. For the training of the network, we use protocols that take advantage of the natural tendency of the system to equilibrate. We illustrate the versatility of our approach via two different platforms: quantum dot networks and enzymatic reaction networks. Both systems can be engineered to have NDC, enabling high performance in standard benchmarks, including sine function approximation and MNIST digit classification. Overall, our work establishes a rigorous link between non-equilibrium steady states and computational expressivity.

</details>


### [500] [Diagonal Adaptive Non-local Observables on Quantum Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15410)
*Huan-Hsin Tseng,Yan Li,Hsin-Yi Lin,Samuel Yen-Chi Chen*

Main category: quant-ph

TL;DR: The paper proposes a simplified form of Adaptive Non-local Observables (ANOs) that reduces computational overhead while retaining the performance benefits of full ANOs in Variational Quantum Algorithms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to make quantum measurement design less computationally expensive while retaining the expanded function space made possible by Adaptive Non-local Observables (ANOs).

Method: The proposed approach restricts ANOs to diagonal observables paired with quantum circuits, which mathematically achieves the same functionality as the full ANO space but with significantly reduced computational complexity.

Result: The diagonal ANO reduces $k$-local observable complexity from $O(4^k)$ to $O(2^k)$, simplifying both parameter optimization and classical computations.

Conclusion: Diagonal ANO offers a computationally efficient alternative to full ANOs, balancing reduced classical optimization costs with similar benefits to expanded quantum function space capabilities.

Abstract: Adaptive Non-local Observables (ANOs) have shown that making quantum observables dynamic can substantially enlarge the function space of Variational Quantum Algorithms, partly shifting hardware demands from circuit synthesis to measurement design. However, this advantage is accompanied by a steep increase in the number of parameters, as well as the classical optimization cost for varying general Hermitian observables.
  We propose a special form of ANO that significantly reduces this burden by considering only diagonal observables paired with quantum circuits. Mathematically, this is equivalent to the full ANO of a large parameter space since diagonal matrices are canonical representatives of the ANO space modulo unitary similarity. As a result, Diagonal ANO retains the same capability of full ANO while reducing $k$-local observable complexity from $O(4^k)$ to $O(2^k)$ and lowering the corresponding measurement-side classical computation. In this sense, diagonal ANO preserves much of the benefit of full ANO while encompassing conventional VQCs as a special case.

</details>


### [501] [Quantum Feature Pyramid Gating for Seismic Image Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15370)
*Taha Gharaibeh,Jyotsna Sharma*

Main category: quant-ph

TL;DR: This paper explores the use of quantum feature gating in hybrid segmentation models for enhancing salt-body delineation in seismic images, achieving significant improvements in accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate salt-body delineation is crucial in seismic interpretation due to its impact on wave propagation, velocity-model complexity, reservoir geometry visibility, and decision-making in exploration and drilling.

Method: The paper introduces a hybrid segmentation framework embedding a parameterized quantum circuit (PQC) for feature fusion within an encoder-decoder pipeline, evaluated across various configurations on the TGS Salt Identification Challenge dataset.

Result: Replacing Quantum FPN Gates with conventional methods significantly reduced mean IoU from 0.9389 to 0.8404; PQC application in skip connections offered a performance boost, evidencing improvement in seismic image segmentation.

Conclusion: Controlled experiments demonstrate that quantum feature gating can enhance pixel-level segmentation in complex geophysical tasks, indicating its potential for broader applications.

Abstract: Accurate salt-body delineation is essential for seismic interpretation because salt structures distort wave propagation, complicate velocity-model building, obscure reservoir geometry, and increase uncertainty in exploration and drilling decisions. Although hybrid quantum-classical models have shown competitive performance on small-scale image-classification tasks, their value for dense, pixel-level geophysical prediction remains largely untested. This work introduces quantum feature gating, a hybrid segmentation architecture that embeds a parameterized quantum circuit (PQC) at feature-fusion points within an encoder-decoder pipeline. A 4-qubit, 2-layer PQC with data re-uploading computes a learned convex combination of lateral and top-down features at each Feature Pyramid Network merge point. A global-average-pooling layer maps encoder features to a fixed 4-dimensional quantum input, decoupling the 72-parameter quantum budget from backbone size and image resolution. The method is evaluated on the 2018 TGS Salt Identification Challenge using 4,000 seismic images at 101 x 101 resolution, across two integration topologies, eight circuit variants, and six encoders with 8M to 118M parameters under five-fold cross-validation. In a controlled EfficientNetV2-L ablation at 256 x 256 resolution, replacing the three Quantum FPN Gates with element-wise addition while holding the encoder, loss schedule, splits, and threshold search fixed reduces mean IoU from 0.9389 to 0.8404, a 9.85 percentage-point gap. Inserting the same circuit as skip-connection attention in a custom U-Net improves IoU by 0.88 points over the SolidUNet baseline, showing that the PQC contribution depends on where and what it gates. These results provide controlled evidence that quantum feature fusion can improve dense seismic segmentation.

</details>


<div id='cs.LO'></div>

# cs.LO [[Back]](#toc)

### [502] [Ensuring Logic in the Fog: Sound POMDP Synthesis with LTL Objectives](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12581)
*Can Zhou,Yulong Gao,Pian Yu*

Main category: cs.LO

TL;DR: This paper introduces a novel reward-shaping mechanism for autonomous agents that helps in solving navigation problems in uncertain, partially observable environments with Linear Temporal Logic (LTL) constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the challenges of navigating partially observable environments under complex temporal constraints using LTL, whose verification and synthesis are difficult due to inherent undecidability.

Method: The authors propose a belief-dependent reward-shaping mechanism for generating rewards based on certified LTL satisfaction. They integrate this into an enhanced Monte Carlo Planning framework to guide agents.

Result: The proposed method outperforms existing solvers in challenging scenarios and demonstrates scalability and effectiveness across diverse benchmark domains.

Conclusion: The research provides a sound and practical method for improving agent navigation under uncertainty with certified LTL satisfaction, offering both reliability and scalability.

Abstract: Synthesising autonomous agents that can navigate uncertain environments while adhering to complex temporal constraints remains a fundamental challenge. While Linear Temporal Logic (LTL) provides a rigorous language for specifying such tasks, the inherent undecidability of qualitatively verifying LTL satisfaction in partially observable Markov decision processes renders quantitative synthesis difficult, especially when designing reliable reward signals for approximate solvers. In this paper, we bridge this gap with a novel, sound reward-shaping mechanism that dynamically generates belief-dependent rewards grounded in certified LTL satisfaction. By integrating this mechanism into an enhanced Monte Carlo Planning framework, we empower agents to navigate the `fog' of partial observability with a search process focused on maximising verifiable success. Our experiments demonstrate that this approach not only thrives in scenarios where existing solvers fail but also maintains effectiveness and scalability across diverse benchmark domains.

</details>


<div id='math.NA'></div>

# math.NA [[Back]](#toc)

### [503] [Proposal-Guided Greedy Surrogate Refinement for PDE-Driven High-Dimensional Rare-Event Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15356)
*Zhiwei Gao,George Karniadakis*

Main category: math.NA

TL;DR: This paper proposes a surrogate-assisted adaptive importance sampling method for high-dimensional rare-event simulations to reduce expensive performance evaluations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-dimensional rare-event simulations often require numerous evaluations of expensive high-fidelity models. Existing methods struggle with cost-efficiently identifying failure-relevant regions in such setups.

Method: The framework employs a surrogate combining an encoder and neural network to refine the model accuracy locally along an evolving proposal distribution in adaptive importance sampling. A greedy latent-space rule selects samples for balanced proximity and diversity.

Result: Numerical experiments show the method substantially reduces high-fidelity evaluations while maintaining accuracy comparable to full-model adaptive importance sampling on benchmarks and PDE problems up to 100 dimensions.

Conclusion: The approach improves computational efficiency and maintains accuracy in high-dimensional rare-event simulations, demonstrating practical feasibility for complex surrogate constructions.

Abstract: Accurate surrogate construction for PDE-driven high-dimensional rare-event simulation is challenging when performance evaluations are expensive. Since a globally accurate surrogate may require many high-fidelity evaluations, adaptive importance sampling provides a natural localization tool: its evolving proposal distribution progressively identifies the failure-relevant region. Motivated by this observation, we propose a surrogate-assisted adaptive importance sampling framework that refines the surrogate locally along the evolving proposal, rather than over the entire input space. The surrogate combines an encoder with a neural network, providing a low-dimensional latent representation for both prediction and sample selection. At each adaptive iteration, candidates drawn from the current proposal are selected by a greedy latent-space rule balancing proximity to the estimated failure boundary and sample diversity. The selected samples are evaluated by the high-fidelity model and used to refine the surrogate, which then guides the subsequent cross-entropy-type adaptive proposal update. We establish one-step proposal stability bounds under local surrogate errors, together with surrogate-induced misclassification and finite-sample estimation error bounds. Numerical experiments on multimodal benchmarks and PDE-driven rare-event problems up to 100 dimensions show that the proposed method achieves accuracy comparable to true-model adaptive importance sampling while requiring substantially fewer high-fidelity evaluations.

</details>


### [504] [Amortized Energy-Based Bayesian Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15407)
*Hojjat Kaveh,Ricardo Baptista,Andrew M. Stuart*

Main category: math.NA

TL;DR: This paper presents a transport-based approach for efficient Bayesian inference in nonlinear inverse problems using joint samples, avoiding traditional costly methods like Markov chain Monte Carlo.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The challenge of solving nonlinear inverse problems using traditional Bayesian methods like MCMC is computationally expensive when repeated inference is required. This motivates the need for an efficient, scalable, and likelihood-free approach.

Method: The paper proposes a transport map that learns how to push forward a reference measure to approximate the posterior distribution. This is trained using an energy-distance objective and avoids complex computations like density evaluations or Jacobian determinants. The map is parameterized in function-space problems and represented via neural operators for infinite dimensions.

Result: The method is applied to three nonlinear inverse problem scenarios, demonstrating the ability to capture posterior structure, manage multimodality, and enable efficient posterior sampling for new observations.

Conclusion: The proposed transport-based approach offers a computationally efficient alternative to traditional methods, showing promise in both finite and infinite-dimensional inverse problems while remaining flexible and scalable.

Abstract: We consider amortized Bayesian inference for nonlinear inverse problems in settings where only samples from the joint distribution of parameters and observations are available. Classical methods such as Markov chain Monte Carlo require solving a new inference problem for each observation, which can be computationally prohibitive when inference must be repeated many times. We propose a transport-based approach that learns an observation-dependent map pushing forward a reference measure to approximate the posterior distribution. The map is trained by minimizing an averaged energy-distance objective between the true posterior and the learned pushforward. This formulation is likelihood-free, requiring only joint samples, and avoids density evaluation, invertibility constraints, and Jacobian determinant computations. For function-space inverse problems with Gaussian priors, we parameterize the transport map as the identity plus a perturbation in the Cameron-Martin space of the prior, preserving absolute continuity with respect to the prior. In infinite-dimensional settings, the map is represented using neural operators. We illustrate the method on a finite-dimensional nonlinear inverse problem and two PDE-constrained inverse problems arising in porous medium flow and seismic inversion. The results show that the learned transport captures posterior structure, including multimodality and dominant modes, while enabling fast posterior sampling for new observations.

</details>


<div id='cs.DS'></div>

# cs.DS [[Back]](#toc)

### [505] [Complexity of Non-Log-Concave Sampling in Fisher Information](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15859)
*Sinho Chewi,Andre Wibisono*

Main category: cs.DS

TL;DR: This paper addresses the challenge of sampling from log-smooth, non-log-concave distributions by providing relative Fisher information guarantees with a proximal sampler algorithm. The method achieves improved query complexity over prior approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to bridge the gap in query complexity between log-concave and non-log-concave sampling, especially under strict relative Fisher information guarantees, which parallels optimization in finding approximate stationary points.

Method: The authors use the proximal sampler with an approximate restricted Gaussian oracle (RGO) based on the recent advancements in log-concave sampling with high-accuracy Rényi divergence guarantees.

Result: The proposed approach achieves query complexity for relative Fisher information that matches the dimension dependence found in log-concave sampling, thus improving previous work on non-log-concave sampling.

Conclusion: The study advances non-log-concave sampling techniques and establishes a direct connection between improvements in its dimension dependence and the advancements in log-concave sampling complexity.

Abstract: We study the query complexity of obtaining a relative Fisher information guarantee for sampling from a log-smooth non-log-concave distribution; this is a sampling analog of finding an approximate stationary point in optimization. Our algorithm is based on the proximal sampler, which is an implicit discretization of the Langevin diffusion, and requires an implementation of the backward step known as the restricted Gaussian oracle (RGO). We show that by leveraging the recent results for log-concave sampling with high-accuracy guarantees in Rényi divergence, we can obtain an approximate RGO implementation that -- when used with the proximal sampler -- yields a complexity guarantee in relative Fisher information that inherits the same dimension dependence as log-concave sampling, and improves upon prior work for non-log-concave sampling. We also show a converse reduction that any improvement in the dimension dependence in relative Fisher information for non-log-concave sampling will yield an improved dimension dependence for high-accuracy log-concave sampling.

</details>


<div id='math.DS'></div>

# math.DS [[Back]](#toc)

### [506] [Symplectic Neural Operators for Learning Infinite Dimensional Hamiltonian Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15881)
*Yeang Makara,Yusuke Tanaka,Takashi Matsubara,Takaharu Yaguchi*

Main category: math.DS

TL;DR: This paper introduces the Symplectic Neural Operator to preserve the symplectic structure in Hamiltonian PDEs, ensuring better energy behavior and stability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard data-driven architectures struggle with maintaining the symplectic structures of Hamiltonian PDEs, prompting a need for specially designed methods.

Method: The paper proposes a neural operator architecture with theoretical foundations for symplectic structure preservation, paired with analyses on stability and learning accuracy through numerical experiments.

Result: The Symplectic Neural Operator demonstrates improved energy behavior and long-term stability compared to non-structure-preserving approaches.

Conclusion: The Symplectic Neural Operator is a promising tool for simulating Hamiltonian PDEs, addressing computational and structural challenges with enhanced stability and energy accuracy.

Abstract: The modeling and simulation of infinite-dimensional Hamiltonian systems are central problems in mathematical physics and engineering, however they pose significant computational and structural challenges for standard data-driven architectures. In this work, we introduce the Symplectic Neural Operator, a neural operator architecture designed to preserve the symplectic structure intrinsic to Hamiltonian PDEs. We provide a theoretical characterization of their symplecticity and establish a rigorous long-term stability result based on the combination of symplectic structure preservation and learning accuracy. Numerical experiments on canonical Hamiltonian PDEs corroborate this theoretical result and show that SNOs exhibit improved energy behavior compared with non-structure-preserving neural operators.

</details>


<div id='physics.chem-ph'></div>

# physics.chem-ph [[Back]](#toc)

### [507] [Reweighting free energy profiles between universal machine learning interatomic potentials for fast consensus building](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15630)
*Sauradeep Majumdar,Miguel Steiner,Johannes C. B. Dietschreit,Swagata Roy,Daniel Willimetz,Lukaš Grajciar,Rafael Gómez-Bombarelli*

Main category: physics.chem-ph

TL;DR: This paper introduces a method to reweight potential of mean force (PMF) derived from machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) to achieve results closer to high-accuracy density functional theory (DFT) calculations, thereby saving computational cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Estimating free energy profiles using DFT is computationally expensive, and although MLIPs reduce this cost, their accuracy is uncertain and dependent on training data. There is a need for an efficient way to obtain reliable PMFs without intensive simulations.

Method: The authors propose a scalable framework that reweights PMFs obtained from a single source MLIP to match target MLIPs by utilizing robust analytical corrections and a mean energy-gap approximation to avoid statistical collapse in large systems.

Result: The method is applied to a 601-atom Li$^+$ transport system. It successfully reproduces high-fidelity target PMFs matching multiple DFT reference levels, while clustering different MLIPs based on their training datasets. High-quality thermodynamic properties are recovered despite low phase-space overlap.

Conclusion: This framework provides an affordable, scalable protocol to achieve cross-model consistency in thermodynamic properties without requiring redundant and costly simulations, offering utility in materials chemistry applications.

Abstract: Free energy profiles serve as a fundamental bridge between microscopic atomic fluctuations and macroscopic thermodynamic observables. Estimating the free energy profile along a reaction coordinate, referred to as the potential of mean force (PMF), with density functional theory (DFT) accuracy is computationally expensive. Universal machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) drastically reduce this cost, but their accuracy is strongly determined by their training data and hence can be uncertain for a given system. In this work, we present a systematic and scalable framework for reweighting PMFs, initially sampled with a single 'source' MLIP, across a representative suite of target MLIPs. Because traditional direct exponential reweighting fails for large system sizes due to low phase-space overlap between potentials, we deploy robust analytical corrections. Applying this to a complex 601-atom system of Li$^+$ transport in a nanoconfined electrolyte, we demonstrate that a mean energy-gap approximation effectively bypasses statistical collapse, producing a highly stable PMF matching the target PMF. Using this approach, we recover high-fidelity target thermodynamics across multiple DFT reference levels (PBE+D3, PBE-sol, r$^2$SCAN,r$^2$SCAN-D4) at a fraction of the computational cost of full simulations. Furthermore, thermodynamic analysis reveals that the studied MLIPs partition into two distinct clusters driven by their training data. Our reweighting framework successfully recovers target thermodynamic properties--specifically, reaction and activation free energies--even when the phase-space overlap between potentials is critically low. Ultimately, this approach establishes a vital diagnostic protocol to achieve affordable cross-model consensus on materials chemistry properties without redundant, resource-intensive simulations.

</details>


<div id='math.ST'></div>

# math.ST [[Back]](#toc)

### [508] [Node-private community estimation in stochastic block models: Tractable algorithms and lower bounds](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15943)
*Laurentiu Marchis,Ethan D'souza,Tomáš Flídr,Po-Ling Loh*

Main category: math.ST

TL;DR: This paper studies community recovery in stochastic block models under differential privacy constraints and proposes novel algorithms for this purpose.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the challenge of ensuring node differential privacy while performing community recovery in graphs, making the algorithms stable under node-wise changes.

Method: The authors develop algorithms that privatize the adjacency matrix, leverage sampling from an exponential mechanism, and construct smooth projections to bounded-degree graphs. They combine these with edge-private algorithms and ensure computational tractability within polynomial time.

Result: The paper provides algorithms that work under node differential privacy, novel lower bounds on the privacy parameter for consistent estimation, and technical insights into scaling private algorithms under high privacy parameters.

Conclusion: The study advances the state-of-the-art for private community recovery by introducing practical algorithms and theoretical insights into node privacy, offering both computational and conceptual contributions.

Abstract: We study the classical problem of community recovery in stochastic block models with a fixed number of communities, with a twist: We seek algorithms that are stable with respect to node-wise changes in the graph structure, formally defined as a differential privacy constraint. The algorithms we develop are based on spectral clustering, where we introduce privacy to the community recovery pipeline in the form of directly privatizing the adjacency matrix; private PCA; private convex optimization; private low-rank matrix estimation; and private approximate subspace estimation. Straightforward applications of existing private algorithms lead to a rapid increase in the privacy parameter $ε$ in order to ensure consistent estimation under node differential privacy, in contrast with the simpler setting of edge privacy. To alleviate these issues, we develop novel algorithms based on (1) sampling from an exponential mechanism with a Lipschitz extension and (2) a general framework for constructing smooth projections from the space of undirected graphs to the space of bounded-degree graphs, which can then be combined with various edge-private algorithms. Importantly, the methods we develop are all computable in polynomial-time as a function of the number of nodes in the graph. We also develop novel lower bounds on the growth rate of $ε$ required in order to achieve consistent community estimation under node privacy. On a technical note, our paper highlights the complications that arise when analyzing private algorithms under the non-standard scaling $ε\rightarrow \infty$ and proposes some solutions. We also provide a novel application of the HGR maximal correlation from information theory in the context of accuracy amplification in PAC learning, which may be of independent interest.

</details>
